exemplar.title: To what extent is synesthesia an innate trait versus an acquired condition?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is synesthesia an innate trait versus an acquired condition?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and precise communication of the topic and its purpose throughout the essay.
A sharply focused research question (âTo what extent is synesthesia an innate trait versus an acquired condition?â) that is consistently revisited.
A well-justified and appropriate range of methodological choices, with clear evidence of informed source selection.
weaknesses: Minor omissions in detailing sample selection procedures (e.g., recruitment criteria) limit transparency of data validity.
Could preview key genetic methods more explicitly to better orient the reader before analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments placed this work firmly in the 5-6 band: the topic is communicated accurately and with clear focus (band 5), the research question is sharply stated and connected to discussion (band 5), and the methodology is complete with appropriate sources and evidence of informed selection (band 5). Minor omissions prevent the full 6, so a score of 5 reflects high performance with room for minor improvement.

strengths: Demonstrates excellent subject knowledge, accurately summarising complex neurogenetic procedures and linking them to theory.
Consistent and precise use of discipline-specific terminology (e.g., concordance, penetrance, HLOD scores, inducer-concurrent).
Strong integration of examples (subtypes of synesthesia) to illustrate breadth of understanding.
weaknesses: Occasional superficial treatment of neuro-mechanistic explanations prevents deeper insight.
A slight overstatement of prevalence (âexceptionally rareâ) suggests a need for more balanced wording.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows âexcellent knowledgeâ with consistently relevant source application and coherent understanding, and uses terminology accurately and consistently (band 5 in both strands). A few superficial treatments and tonal oversights keep it just below the perfect band, warranting a score of 5 out of 6.

strengths: Excellent selection of varied, peer-reviewed studies clearly relevant to the research question.
Well-supported critiques of study designs (e.g., sample size, zygosity testing, recall bias).
Insightful positioning of structural neural changes as consequences rather than prerequisites, showing nuanced evaluation.
weaknesses: Analysis synthesis remains somewhat fragmentedâevidence is evaluated study-by-study rather than fully integrated.
Deeper reporting of inferential statistics (p-values, confidence intervals) and typical thresholds (e.g., LOD > 3) would enhance credibility.
The final discussion could weigh evidence more decisively rather than restating complexity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality consistently meets the top-band description (7-9), and the depth of analysis and evaluation falls in the mid 4-6 bands for synthesis and critical engagement. A holistic score of 9 (out of 12) recognises strong, relevant research and clear supported analysis while reflecting limitations in integration and statistical contextualisation.

strengths: Meets all structural conventions (title page, abstract, contents, headings, figures, pagination) with logical flow.
Consistent formatting: word count, section headings, in-text citations, and reference list adhere closely to IB/APA guidelines.
Tables and figures are clearly labelled and cross-checked against the text and contents page.
weaknesses: Could add sub-entries in the Table of Contents for quicker navigation between key subsections.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation fully satisfies the top-band requirements in both structure and layout/formatting (band 3-4 in both strands). Minor suggestions for navigational improvements do not detract from the exemplary formatting, justifying the maximum score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Technical termsâconcordance, penetrance, inducer-concurrent, HLOD, Stroop effectâare deployed accurately and consistently throughout.  Definitions are provided where needed, and terminology remains discipline-appropriate.  Minor typographical errors do not detract from conceptual precision, justifying a top-band mark.

Formatting is consistently applied: word count provided, title stated, page numbers and table of contents match pagination, in-text citations and full reference list adhere closely to APA formatting.  Minor spacing/typographic glitches do not impede clarity, warranting the highest layout band.

The essay demonstrates strong command of psychological and neurogenetic concepts, accurately summarising complex procedures (HLOD scores, SNP arrays) and linking them to theory.  Citations are consistently integrated and relevant.  Occasional superficial treatment of neuro-mechanistic explanations (e.g., reduced inhibition vs. hyper-connectivity) prevents a perfect score but overall knowledge is excellent.

The student outlines clear methodological choices (twin/kinship, adoption, environmental experiments) and justifies them in relation to the RQ.  Selection of recent peer-reviewed studies, use of consistency tests, and focus on a defined time-frame show informed selection.  Minor omissions (e.g., no primary data collection) keep the work from exemplary, but the range and rationale remain appropriate and complete.

The student introduces synaesthesia clearly, defines the phenomenon with precise citations, and states a purposeâdetermining the balance of innate versus acquired influences.  Background context, prevalence figures and cognitive significance are concisely explained, making the focus immediately apparent.  Minor lapses (e.g., prevalence figure treated as âexceptionally rareâ without nuance) prevent a flawless impression, but overall communication is accurate and purposeful, aligning best with the upper-band description.

The body draws on multiple peer-reviewed genetic and environmental studies, each clearly relevant to the RQ.  Source selection is varied (twin, SNP, training, toy studies) and mostly up-to-date.  Some reliance on small-sample research is acknowledged, showing awareness of limitations.  This breadth and relevance fit the 7-9 band; slight unevenness in geographical diversity holds it to the mid-point (8).

The research questionââTo what extent is synesthesia an innate trait versus an acquired condition?ââis unambiguous, sharply delimited and explicitly revisited in the discussion and conclusion.  All selected studies map directly onto this focus.  Although the phrasing is broad (âto what extentâ), the ensuing analysis keeps the scope manageable, demonstrating effective connection to the body of the essay.

The essay follows IB EE conventions: title page, abstract information, contents, introduction-method-results-discussion-conclusion-references.  Headings are logical, tables/figures are labelled, and pagination is present.  No required element appears missing; therefore the top presentation band is appropriate.

The discussion recognises conflicting evidence, researcher bias, and the need for multimodal approaches, indicating partial critical evaluation.  However, the argument stops short of fully integrating findings into a nuanced stance; the conclusion merely restates complexity without weighing evidence strength.  This positions the work comfortably in the 4-6 band at 5 marks.

The student provides structured critiques of each study, noting sample size, zygosity testing method, recall bias, etc.  There is genuine analytical comment, but synthesis across studies is limited; contrasting evidence is listed more than interwoven.  Consequently analysis is âclearâ¦mostly supported,â matching the 4-6 band, awarded a solid 5.

annotations.comment: The historical overview of innate versus acquired theories provides clear context and frames the research direction, showing a strong foundation in Focus and Method.
The âInvestigation Methodologyâ section is clearly demarcated with a descriptive header, supporting readability and logical flow.
While the methodology is well-explained, the sample selection process (e.g., recruitment criteria) could be detailed further to strengthen the justification of data validity.

The title âDevelopment of Synesthesiaâ is concise and informative, effectively guiding the reader into the investigationâs focus. The clear presentation aligns well with IB conventions, aiding navigation.

The research question âTo what extent is synesthesia an innate trait versus an acquired condition?â is sharply stated and immediately framed, providing a clear focus that is consistently revisited throughout the essay.
Word count is correctly provided at the start of the report, demonstrating adherence to IB formatting requirements and ensuring the investigation stays within the 4,000-word limit.
The Table of Contents is comprehensive and logically organized, accurately reflecting all major sections and enhancing the readerâs ability to navigate the investigation.
The table entries align precisely with page numbers, indicating careful cross-checking and reinforcing structural clarity.
Consider adding sub-entries for key subsections within major sections to improve quick referencing for readers seeking specific methodological or analytical details.
The definition of synesthesia is thorough and well-cited, demonstrating clear command of technical terminology such as âinducerâ and âconcurrentâ and reinforcing subject understanding.
Describing a 4.4% prevalence as âexceptionally rareâ overstates the phenomenon; consider framing it as ârelatively uncommonâ to maintain precision and avoid unbalanced tone.
Integration of examples of various synesthesia subtypes strengthens understanding and illustrates the breadth of the phenomenon effectively.
The âGenetic Studiesâ header introduces the upcoming analysis cleanly, but consider previewing which key genetic methods are prioritized to orient the reader further.
Highlighting higher concordance in same-sex versus opposite-sex DZ twins insightfully points to environmental influences, illustrating nuanced critical thinking.
The rationale for including environmental and genetic studies is clearly articulated, aligning methodology directly with the research question.
Restating the research question within the methodology section effectively ties procedural choices back to the investigationâs focus.
The twin study description provides a comprehensive account of IV and DV definitions, supporting replicability and clarity in the research design.
Results are clearly summarized, but reporting of inferential statistics (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) would enhance the depth and credibility of the analysis.
Table 1 effectively displays concordance rates for MZ and DZ twins, enabling quick comparison and reinforcing the genetic argument visually.
The concise summary of genetic heterogeneity across families effectively bridges findings and highlights variability, demonstrating clear synthesis.
Table 2âs subgroup analysis is valuable, but with only 4 and 7 pairs, the reliability of these percentages is limited; consider discussing confidence intervals or caution in interpretation.
The critique of zygosity self-reporting is pertinent; expanding on how this might bias concordance estimates would deepen the critical evaluation.
The detailed account of SNP array and linkage analysis methods demonstrates excellent grasp of advanced genetic techniques.
Reporting HLOD scores is informative, but the essay could explain typical significance thresholds (e.g., LOD > 3) to contextualize the findings.
The recognition of incomplete penetrance and genetic heterogeneity is insightful; linking this discussion to broader models (e.g., polygenic inheritance) would further strengthen the argument.
Discussion of variable expression across synesthesia subtypes is valuable; further distinguishing particular subtype mechanisms would deepen subject understanding.
Positioning structural neural changes as a consequence rather than a prerequisite offers a nuanced perspective that enriches critical evaluation.
